summary: This source provides a brief biographical overview of Hildegard of Bingen, highlighting her roles as a polymath, composer, and medical writer. It details her unique interpretation of humoral theory, which relies heavily on the microcosm-macrocosm analogy and the hierarchical balance of the four elements and humors.

principles.statement: Disease arises when subordinate humors improperly dominate the body.
principles.rationale: Health requires a hierarchical inter-balance of the four humors, which correspond to celestial and terrestrial elements. When terrestrial humors overpower celestial ones, illness occurs.
principles.source_quote: disease-causing imbalance of these humors resulted from the improper dominance of the subordinate humors.

mental_models.name: Microcosm-Macrocosm Analogy
mental_models.description: Understanding the human body (microcosm) by mapping it to the universe (macrocosm) through interrelated patterns of four, such as elements, seasons, and humors.
mental_models.example: Associating blood and phlegm with the 'celestial' elements of fire and air, and the two biles with the 'terrestrial' elements of water and earth.

signature_quotes.text: the four elements (fire, air, water, and earth), the four seasons, the four humors, the four zones of the earth, and the four major winds.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the interrelated patterns of four in her medical and scientific writings.

anti_patterns.description: Allowing subordinate humors to improperly dominate.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It disrupts the hierarchical inter-balance of the body's humors, leading to disease.
